results.title: Frustrated with LLMs: Not the right path to AGI | Alfonso R. Reyes posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content: # Coding and Research with Automated Interactive Agents. Part X Exactly right! I deal with it every day. And it’s not only OpenAI; Claude as well. It is very frustrating when very good code work gets destroyed by the continuous guessing of the LLM models. They will never admit they don’t know. They continue spitting bad code, or patches, saying this time will work. I said few posts ago that those mistakes are not hallucinations but rather reflection of the way they have been designed. It is becoming more evident by the day that this AI LLMs is no the right path to AGI. No matter how much data you throw at the models, they are far from evolving in something better. I believe that LLMs have plateaued, and it’s becoming more common to find people with bad experiences with these agents. But [...] to find people with bad experiences with these agents. But they are not totally useless. They help in some way to get something started until they lose focus, or their attention becomes erratic. They tend to forget the experiences and lessons learned from one session to another. Even in the same session, they don’t remember they wrote that code, and finger point the user for writing such a bad code. But if LLMs are not the right approach to AI, then what is? Although none of it is my money, I wonder if investors behind this version of AI are aware of the consequences of betting so many hundreds of billions of dollars in data centers that will be of ephemeral life.

results.title: 10 things I learned from burning myself out with AI coding agents
results.content:
The ability of AI agents to use trial and error—attempting something and then trying again—helps mitigate the brittleness of LLMs somewhat. But it’s not perfect, and it can be frustrating to see a coding agent spin its wheels trying and failing at a task repeatedly, either because it doesn’t know how to do it or because it previously learned how to solve a problem but then forgot because the context window got compacted (more on that here).

Violent Checkers is a physics-based corruption of the classic board game, coded using Claude Code. [...] To get around this, it helps to have the AI model take copious notes as it goes along about how it solved certain problems so that future instances of the agent can learn from them again. You also want to set ground rules in the claude.md file that the agent reads when it begins its session.

This brittleness means that coding agents are almost frighteningly good at what they’ve been trained and fine-tuned on—modern programming languages, JavaScript, HTML, and similar well-represented technologies—and generally terrible at tasks on which they have not been deeply trained, such as 6502 Assembly or programming an Atari 800 game with authentic-looking character graphics. [...] ## 7. Even fast isn’t fast enough

While using Claude Code for a while, it’s easy to take for granted that you suddenly have the power to create software without knowing certain programming languages. This is amazing at first, but you can quickly become frustrated that what is conventionally a very fast development process isn’t fast enough. Impatience at the coding machine sets in, and you start wanting more.

But even if you do know the programming languages being used, you don’t get a free pass. You still need to make key decisions about how the project will unfold. And when the agent gets stuck or makes a mess of things, your programming knowledge becomes essential for diagnosing what went wrong and steering it back on course.

## 8. People may become busier than ever

results.title: Most developers waste months learning AI coding agents the hard way, making the same critical mistakes that kill productivity and lead to frustrating coding sessions. After analyzing hundreds of… | Sahar Mor
results.content:
Most developers waste months learning AI coding agents the hard way, making the same critical mistakes that kill productivity and lead to frustrating coding sessions. After analyzing hundreds of developer workflows across tools like Cursor, Claude Code, and other AI coding agents, a clear pattern emerges: the same fundamental mistakes sabotage productivity regardless of which coding agent/IDE you choose. Here are a few of these crucial mistakes: (1) Skipping the overview phase - don't let AI generate code immediately without understanding its planned approach first. (2) Assuming AI understanding - don’t assume the AI has correctly understood all requirements without verification. Avoid discovering, after hours of coding, that your agent misinterpreted core requirements from the start. [...] your agent misinterpreted core requirements from the start. That’s why I often append “Ask clarifying questions as needed” in the planning phase, i.e. my back-and-forth interaction before implementing a feature. (3) Avoiding large feature drops - don't request or implement large feature sets at once. Break complex features into smaller, manageable chunks. It takes time, but eventually you build the granularity intuition on what is “small enough”. (4) Maintaining long sessions without resets - don't run extended AI sessions without periodic context resets. As conversations grow longer, agent context degrades, leading to increasingly irrelevant suggestions. Run /compact or start a new chat session in Cursor. 138 other examples here  More tips on coding with AI [...] # Sahar Mor’s Post

Sahar Mor

## Cole Medin
213000 subscribers
371 likes

### Description
11429 views
Posted: 30 Mar 2026
Your AI coding agents fail on complex tasks not because the model is insufficient, but because you aren't separating the planning, building, and evaluation into distinct roles. The building and evaluation agents essentially have to fight each other to get the best results.

Anthropic just published a deep engineering article breaking down exactly how they solved this for multi-hour coding sessions with a GAN-inspired three-agent architecture, and the pattern is something you're going to want to try on your own codebases! results.title: 9 Critical Failure Patterns of Coding Agents | DAPLab
results.content:
### 1: Presentation & UI Grounding Mismatch

Agents struggle to translate visual requests into code. Since they cannot see the interface, they often fail to understand spatial requests, like moving a button, resizing a component, or aligning items. This leads to layouts and styles that do not match what the user intended.

Example: The user asks the agent to build a calendar and display it as a grid. The agent generates the correct calendar data, but displays the days in a single vertical line instead.

### 2: State Management Failures [...] Example: A user interacts with a coding agent that has access to both public and private codebases. The agent fails to separate roles for a ‘regular user’ and an ‘admin,’ and accidentally provides answers from a private admin codebase to regular users, exposing internal data.

### 7: Repeated Code

Agents often write repeated code, adding duplicate functions with very similar logic rather than abstracting it. This leads to a confusing and unmaintainable codebase, making debugging harder for both the user and agent.

Example: The agent duplicates the API setup code in both fetchUserData and fetchProductData instead of using a shared helper. This creates a maintenance burden: if the API token changes, the user must manually find and update it in every single function. [...] ### 2: State Management Failures

As users interact with an application, the system must maintain the in-memory state that is reflected in the interface—for example, the contents of a shopping cart, the ordering of list items, or the current step in an installation flow. The agent often fails to manage shared state between components when refactoring and struggles to even understand what state to store and maintain.

Example: In a to-do list app, a user drags Task 1 below Task 3 to reorder items. The agent updates part of the state but fails to reconcile the full list order, resulting in the wrong order.

### 3: Business Logic Mismatch
